questions.scenario: A developer says, `We have Azure SQL automated backups, so recovery is done.` A bad release wrote wrong order statuses for 20 minutes. What is missing from that statement?

options.text: The restore time, restored database target, app cutover plan, data repair plan, and validation evidence.
Only a nicer database name, because backups automatically update the app.
Only a larger App Service plan, because compute capacity restores bad rows.
Nothing, because every backup is already a complete recovery plan.

questions.explanation: A backup is a restore source. Recovery also needs a target, a cutover path, validation, and a decision about valid data written after the restore point.

questions.scenario: Azure SQL point-in-time restore creates `orders-restored-20260517-1020`. What should the team remember?

options.text: The restored database is a target the app must be pointed to or used for repair; it does not automatically replace application behavior.
The restore deletes all later production data automatically.
The restored database means RTO is zero because the database name exists.
The app can ignore secrets and connection strings after a restore.

questions.explanation: The restore gives the team a database target. The recovery plan still has to decide how the app uses it and how the team handles data after the chosen restore point.

questions.scenario: A receipt blob is deleted by mistake. The storage account has blob soft delete with a 14-day retention period.

options.text: The team may be able to recover the blob within the retention window, but the recovery process and verification still matter.
The blob can be recovered forever, no matter how retention is configured.
Soft delete only protects SQL rows and has no relationship to blobs.
The app automatically switches users to a recovered receipt without any restore action.

questions.explanation: Soft delete can help with accidental deletion during its retention window. The team still needs a process to restore the object and prove users can read the right receipt.

questions.scenario: Which items belong in a recovery plan instead of only in a backup inventory?

options.text: What data or workflow must come back first.
Which restore target is created and how the app uses it.
What proves recovery worked, such as checkout tests, receipt reads, metrics, and data checks.
A vague note that backups exist somewhere.

questions.explanation: A recovery plan names priority, target, app cutover or repair, and validation. A vague backup inventory does not tell the team how users get a working service back.
